mockBank.explanation: Transcatheter Aortic Valve Implantation (TAVI) is a catheter-based (keyhole) procedure for replacing a stenosed aortic valve without open-heart surgery. A stent with a tissue valve is deployed via the femoral artery. It is used especially in high-risk surgical patients. Transcatheter edge-to-edge repair (e.g. MitraClip) is used for mitral regurgitation, not aortic stenosis.

mockBank.question: A patient with a known history of angina attends for a tooth extraction. During the procedure they develop central chest tightness. After sitting the patient upright, what is the NEXT most appropriate action?
options: A. Continue the extraction and administer IV morphine
B. Administer GTN spray sublingually and monitor
C. Immediately call 999 and administer aspirin 300 mg
D. Administer supplemental oxygen and complete the procedure
E. Give IM adrenaline 0.5 mg and transfer to hospital
mockBank.explanation: The first step is to administer GTN (glyceryl trinitrate) spray sublingually, which should relieve angina within minutes by dilating coronary arteries. The patient should be sat comfortably and the procedure stopped. If pain is not relieved within 5 minutes with repeat GTN, or if it is more severe than usual, treat as a possible MI — call 999, give aspirin 300 mg chewed, and administer oxygen if hypoxic. Adrenaline is for anaphylaxis.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following adverse effects of beta-blockers is of direct relevance to oral health?
mockBank.explanation: Beta-blockers are associated with increased tooth demineralisation. Beta-blockers can also rarely cause lichenoid eruptions in the mouth. Gingival hyperplasia is associated with dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers (e.g. nifedipine) and ciclosporin. Dry cough is an ACE inhibitor side effect. Oral ulceration is associated with nicorandil. These drug–oral health associations are high-yield exam topics.

mockBank.question: A patient with hypertension is prescribed adrenaline-containing local anaesthetic for a dental extraction. Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate consideration?
options: A. Adrenaline is absolutely contraindicated in all patients with hypertension
B. A maximum of 3 cartridges of 1:80,000 adrenaline is safe in most patients with controlled hypertension
C. Adrenaline-free local anaesthetics must always be used in hypertensive patients
D. Adrenaline should only be used in hypertensive patients with prior cardiologist approval
E. Adrenaline is contraindicated in hypertensive patients taking beta-blockers due to risk of asystole
mockBank.explanation: Adrenaline-containing local anaesthetic is safe in most patients with controlled hypertension using standard amounts (up to 3 cartridges of 1:80,000). Careful aspiration and slow injection technique minimise inadvertent intravascular injection. Adrenaline is not absolutely contraindicated in hypertensive patients but should be used cautiously in uncontrolled or severe hypertension. The theoretical risk with non-selective beta-blockers is hypertension from unopposed alpha-stimulation (not asystole).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST describes the difference between angina pectoris and acute myocardial infarction (MI)?
options: A. Angina causes ST elevation on ECG; MI does not
B. Angina is irreversible myocardial necrosis; MI is reversible ischaemia
C. Angina is reversible myocardial ischaemia; MI is irreversible myocardial necrosis
D. Angina only occurs in the left ventricle; MI occurs in both ventricles
E. Angina is caused by coronary embolism; MI is caused by coronary atherosclerosis
mockBank.explanation: Angina involves reversible myocardial ischaemia — insufficient blood supply causes chest pain, but blood flow is restored (with rest or GTN) before permanent damage occurs. In MI, complete and sustained occlusion of a coronary artery leads to irreversible myocardial necrosis. MI classically presents with severe chest pain at rest lasting more than 20 minutes, sweating, and nausea. Troponin is released into the bloodstream from necrotic myocytes.

mockBank.question: What is haemopericardium and when is a patient at greatest risk of this complication following MI?
options: A. Blood in the peritoneal cavity; greatest risk on the first day
B. Blood in the pericardial sac; greatest risk at 4–6 days post-MI
C. Blood in the pleural cavity; greatest risk in the first hour of MI
D. Blood in the pericardial sac; greatest risk in the first 24 hours post-MI
E. Fluid around the heart due to inflammation; greatest risk at 3 months
mockBank.explanation: Haemopericardium is blood in the pericardial sac, typically resulting from rupture of the necrotic ventricular wall following MI. The greatest risk is at 4–6 days post-MI, when the infarcted area is at its softest (before fibrosis occurs). The blood fills the pericardial space, compressing the heart (cardiac tamponade), leading to arrhythmia and sudden death. It is a rare but life-threatening complication.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following is the MOST important reason for taking multiple blood pressure readings on separate occasions before diagnosing hypertension?
options: A. BP equipment is inaccurate on first use
B. Blood pressure shows natural variation throughout the day and anxiety in clinic ('white coat effect') can artificially elevate readings
C. First readings are always artificially low due to patient relaxation
D. Multiple readings are needed to identify the diastolic pressure accurately
E. NHS guidelines require three readings before prescribing any medication
mockBank.explanation: Blood pressure shows considerable variation throughout the day and is affected by physical activity, anxiety, stress, caffeine, and position. 'White coat hypertension' — elevated BP in a clinical setting due to anxiety — is well recognised. Taking multiple readings on at least two separate occasions (and using ambulatory or home BP monitoring) avoids over-diagnosing hypertension and unnecessary treatment. ABPM is the gold standard for confirming a diagnosis.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following best describes the sinoatrial (SA) node?
options: A. It is located in the lower part of the right ventricle
B. It is the natural pacemaker of the heart, located high in the right atrium
C. It sends electrical impulses directly to the Purkinje fibres without passing through the AV node
D. It generates impulses at a rate of 100–150 beats per minute at rest
E. Its firing is detected on ECG as the QRS complex
mockBank.explanation: The sinoatrial (SA) node is the natural pacemaker of the heart, located high in the right atrium. It generates impulses at approximately 60–100 beats per minute at rest. These impulses spread through both atria (producing the P wave on ECG), are received by the AV node, and transmitted to the ventricles via the Bundle of His and Purkinje fibres (producing the QRS complex).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following types of embolism results in SYSTEMIC arterial occlusion despite originating in the venous system?
mockBank.explanation: A paradoxical embolism is one that passes from the venous to the arterial circulation, typically through a patent foramen ovale (PFO) — a congenital communication between the right and left atria that normally closes at birth. If a DVT embolises and passes through a PFO into the left heart, it can reach the cerebral or peripheral circulation, causing ischaemic stroke or limb ischaemia. This is why young patients with cryptogenic stroke are investigated for PFO.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following drug combinations for hypertension is specifically associated with an INCREASED risk of developing new-onset diabetes mellitus?
options: A. ACE inhibitor + calcium channel blocker
D. Calcium channel blocker + ACE inhibitor
mockBank.explanation: The combination of a beta-blocker (B) and a thiazide-like diuretic (D) is a 'bad' drug combination in hypertension management because both drug classes independently impair glucose metabolism, and together they significantly increase the risk of new-onset type 2 diabetes mellitus. This combination should be avoided where possible. The NICE ABCD guidelines specifically warn against B+D combinations for this reason.

mockBank.question: HACEK organisms are responsible for approximately 5% of cases of infective endocarditis. Which ONE of the following organisms is included in the HACEK group?
mockBank.explanation: HACEK stands for: Haemophilus spp., Aggregatibacter actinomycetemcomitans (AA), Cardiobacterium hominis, Eikenella corrodens, and Kingella spp. These are fastidious, slow-growing Gram-negative bacteria that are part of the normal oral and upper respiratory flora and account for approximately 5% of IE cases. Aggregatibacter actinomycetemcomitans is also associated with aggressive periodontitis, making it relevant in dentistry.

mockBank.question: What is diapedesis in the context of atherosclerosis development?
options: A. Formation of a lipid lake within a plaque
B. Calcification of the tunica media
C. Movement of white blood cells through the vessel wall into the intima
D. Rupture of an atherosclerotic plaque
E. Migration of smooth muscle cells from the tunica media
mockBank.explanation: Diapedesis is the process by which white blood cells (specifically monocytes) pass through the vessel wall — squeezing between endothelial cells — into the arterial intima. In atherosclerosis, endothelial dysfunction allows monocytes to adhere to and then migrate through the endothelium, where they differentiate into macrophages and take up oxidised LDL to become foam cells (the characteristic feature of the fatty streak).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following antiplatelet drugs irreversibly inhibits platelet cyclooxygenase (COX), reducing thromboxane A2 production?
mockBank.explanation: Aspirin irreversibly acetylates platelet cyclooxygenase-1 (COX-1), permanently inhibiting the production of thromboxane A2 — a potent platelet aggregator and vasoconstrictor. Since platelets cannot synthesise new COX-1 (they have no nucleus), the antiplatelet effect lasts the lifespan of the platelet (7–10 days). Clopidogrel and ticagrelor inhibit the P2Y12 ADP receptor. Dipyridamole inhibits phosphodiesterase. Warfarin is an anticoagulant, not an antiplatelet.

mockBank.question: Cor pulmonale is defined as which ONE of the following?
options: A. Left ventricular failure due to ischaemic heart disease
B. Right heart failure secondary to pulmonary hypertension caused by lung disease
C. Combined left and right heart failure following MI
D. Pericardial effusion causing cardiac tamponade
mockBank.explanation: Cor pulmonale is right ventricular failure caused by pulmonary hypertension secondary to chronic lung disease (most commonly COPD). Sustained hypoxia causes pulmonary vasoconstriction, increasing right ventricular afterload, leading eventually to right heart failure. It presents with peripheral oedema, raised JVP, and signs of the underlying lung disease. It differs from right heart failure caused by left heart disease.

mockBank.question: When prescribing NSAIDs (e.g. ibuprofen) to a patient on warfarin, which ONE of the following explains why this combination is particularly hazardous?
options: A. NSAIDs reduce the absorption of warfarin from the gut
B. NSAIDs displace warfarin from protein binding sites AND inhibit platelet function, markedly increasing bleeding risk
C. NSAIDs increase hepatic metabolism of warfarin, reducing its anticoagulant effect
D. NSAIDs increase renal clearance of warfarin
E. NSAIDs stimulate the synthesis of vitamin K-dependent clotting factors
mockBank.explanation: NSAIDs are dangerous in patients on warfarin for two reasons: (1) they can displace warfarin from plasma protein binding, transiently increasing free warfarin levels; and (2) they inhibit platelet function by inhibiting thromboxane A2 synthesis, impairing the primary haemostatic plug. Additionally, NSAIDs can cause gastrointestinal mucosal damage and bleeding. The combined effect dramatically increases bleeding risk. NSAIDs and COX-2 inhibitors should be avoided in patients on warfarin.

mockBank.question: Lines of Zahn are a histological feature that can confirm a blood clot formed IN VIVO (during life) rather than post-mortem. Which ONE of the following BEST describes these lines?
options: A. Concentric rings of calcified calcium within an atheromatous plaque
B. Alternating light bands (fibrin/platelets) and dark bands (red blood cells) in an organised thrombus
C. Longitudinal fibrous bands seen in organised chronic thrombus
D. Inflammatory cell infiltration around the edges of a thrombus
E. Cholesterol crystal deposits within a fibrolipid plaque
mockBank.explanation: Lines of Zahn are alternating pale layers (fibrin and platelets) and darker layers (red blood cells) seen histologically in a thrombus that formed in flowing blood during life. This laminated appearance results from the alternating deposition of platelets/fibrin and red cells as the clot builds up in the flow of blood. Post-mortem clots (coagula) do not show lines of Zahn — they have a more uniform red/yellow 'chicken fat' appearance.

mockBank.question: Aortic dissection is MOST commonly associated with which ONE of the following underlying conditions?
options: C. Hypertension (in older patients) or connective tissue disorders (in younger patients)
D. Atrial fibrillation and cardiac failure
E. Autoimmune disease and rheumatoid arthritis
mockBank.explanation: Aortic dissection — an intimal tear allowing blood to enter the arterial wall, creating a false lumen — is most commonly caused by hypertension in older patients (leading to medial degeneration) and connective tissue disorders (e.g. Marfan's syndrome, Ehlers-Danlos) in younger patients. Rare causes include trauma (e.g. road traffic accidents) and physiological changes in pregnancy. It presents with sudden, severe, tearing chest/back pain radiating to the scapulae.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following ECG changes is MOST characteristic of acute ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI)?
options: A. Broad QRS complexes with a rate of 150 bpm
B. Absent P waves with an irregular R-R interval
C. ST segment elevation in leads overlying the infarcted territory
E. Tall, peaked T waves in multiple leads
mockBank.explanation: The hallmark ECG change of STEMI is ST segment elevation in leads overlying the territory of the infarcted myocardium (e.g. inferior MI shows elevation in leads II, III, aVF; anterior MI in V1–V4). This indicates transmural ischaemia. Absent P waves with irregular R-R interval is AF. Broad QRS at 150 bpm suggests ventricular tachycardia. Prolonged PR interval is first-degree heart block. Tall peaked T waves can indicate hyperkalaemia.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following is the MOST common cause of mitral stenosis?
options: B. Rheumatic fever causing valve scarring
C. Infective endocarditis destroying the valve leaflets
mockBank.explanation: Mitral stenosis is most commonly caused by rheumatic fever (rheumatic heart disease) — an inflammatory condition following Group A Streptococcal pharyngitis that causes scarring and thickening of the mitral valve leaflets, reducing the valve orifice area. It is now rare in developed countries but remains common globally. Calcific degeneration causes aortic stenosis predominantly. Infective endocarditis more commonly causes regurgitation than stenosis.
